---
title: "Computing at Scale: MapReduce & Spark"
unit: "II"
lesson: "06"
type: concept
tags: [big-data, mapreduce, spark, pyspark, distributed, three-vs]
difficulty: intermediate
duration: "50 mins"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

- **Identify** the Vs of big data and what breaks when data outgrows one machine. *(Understand)*
- **Trace** a computation through map, shuffle, and reduce. *(Analyze)*
- **Use** PySpark in local mode to run a DataFrame aggregation and read its lazy plan. *(Apply)*

> **Where this sits:** after **L05 — NoSQL** · closes Unit II (Big Data).

> **Hands-on:** [Lab — MapReduce by Hand, then PySpark](l06_lab_mapreduce_pyspark.md) ·
> [![Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/{{< var repo.org >}}/{{< var repo.name >}}/blob/{{< var repo.branch >}}/u02_big_data/l06_lab_mapreduce_pyspark.ipynb)

## Why This Matters

L01's FAQ made a promise: *"Do I need big data tools? No — most questions fit on a
laptop. We'll see where that line is in Unit II."* This lesson is that line. L03's
benchmark crunched a million rows in milliseconds on one machine — so what genuinely
requires more? The answer shaped a whole industry: when one machine isn't enough, you
**split the work, compute locally, and assemble the answer** — and you should know both
how that works and when it's overkill.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
A census of Puerto Rico. One option: a single, very fast clerk reads every form on the
island (scale **up** — a bigger machine). The other: each of the 78 municipalities counts
its own forms locally (**map**), mails one subtotal per question to the capital
(**shuffle**), where they're added up (**reduce**) (scale **out** — more machines).
MapReduce is the 78-clerks idea, formalized.
:::

## The Vs and the Single-Machine Ceiling

"Big data" classically means data challenging on three axes (the original trio):

| V | The challenge | What breaks on one machine |
|---|--------------|----------------------------|
| **Volume** | too much data | doesn't fit on disk or in RAM; one pass takes days |
| **Velocity** | arriving too fast | ingest can't keep up; analysis is stale before it ends |
| **Variety** | mixed shapes at once | one schema can't hold tables + JSON + free text (L01's taxonomy, L04-L05 lived it) |

A fourth V is commonly appended — and it's the one this course's syllabus emphasizes:
**veracity**, quality that varies wildly across sources. At scale it bites hardest of
all, because cleaning (already most of the work, per L01) multiplies with every source
you add.

Two honest observations before reaching for a cluster. First, **the ceiling is high**:
one modern machine with DuckDB handles tens of gigabytes comfortably (L02/L03 were not
toys). Second, when you do cross the ceiling, *moving* the data becomes the enemy —
copying 40 TB to a laptop is hours of network time before any analysis. The cure inverts
the flow: **move the computation to where the data lives**.

## Splitting the Work: HDFS and MapReduce

The storage half is a **distributed file system** (Hadoop's is HDFS): one huge file is
chopped into blocks, the blocks spread across many machines, each block stored on more
than one (machines fail; the data must survive). Now the question: how do you compute on
a file that lives in pieces on fifty machines?

**MapReduce** answers with a discipline of three phases:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    F[Input, split in chunks] --> M1[Map worker 1]
    F --> M2[Map worker 2]
    F --> M3[Map worker 3]
    M1 --> S[Shuffle: route pairs by key]
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    S --> R1[Reduce: key group A]
    S --> R2[Reduce: key group B]
    R1 --> O[Assembled answer]
    R2 --> O
```

Reading the diagram: **map** runs on each chunk *where it is stored* — no data movement,
full parallelism — turning records into `(key, value)` pairs. **Shuffle** is the only
phase that moves data: every pair travels to the worker responsible for its key. **Reduce**
collapses each key's bucket into a result, again in parallel. Your `GROUP BY` from L02
secretly had this shape all along — map (emit group key), shuffle (gather groups), reduce
(aggregate) — which is why the lab can write MapReduce in fifteen lines of plain Python
before touching any framework.

## Spark: MapReduce, Industrialized

Writing raw map/reduce jobs (Hadoop's programming model) proved verbose and slow —
every intermediate result hit disk. **Apache Spark** kept the paradigm and fixed the
ergonomics:

| | Hadoop MapReduce | Spark |
|---|------------------|-------|
| You write | map and reduce functions, by hand | DataFrame operations (`groupBy`, `agg`) — SQL-shaped, L02 skills transfer |
| Intermediate data | written to disk between phases | kept in **memory** across steps |
| Execution | eager, one job at a time | **lazy**: builds a plan, optimizes it, runs on demand |
| Today | legacy (its filesystem, HDFS, lives on) | the de facto standard for cluster compute |

Two Spark words you'll meet in the lab. The **driver** is your notebook process — it
holds the plan; **executors** are the workers that hold data partitions and do the
computing. And **lazy evaluation**: `groupBy(...).agg(...)` returns instantly because
*nothing has run* — Spark just recorded the plan. Only an **action** (`.show()`,
`.count()`, a write) triggers execution, which lets Spark optimize the whole pipeline
first (you'll read one of those optimized plans with `.explain()` — and see it pull the
L03 trick of reading only the columns the query needs).

## When NOT to Use a Cluster

- **Stay on the laptop when:** the data fits on one machine (rule of thumb: up to tens
  of GB with DuckDB/pandas), the job runs comfortably overnight, or you're iterating on
  a sample anyway. Spark's startup and shuffle overhead make it *slower* than DuckDB at
  laptop scale — clusters buy *capacity*, not free speed.
- **Scale out when:** the data genuinely exceeds one machine's disk/RAM, a pipeline must
  finish faster than one machine allows, or the data already lives in a distributed
  store and moving it out costs more than computing in place.
- The professional default order: **sample it, then laptop, then one big machine, then
  cluster** — and most projects never reach the last step.

## Quiz Hooks

*Feeds the retrieval quiz at the start of the next session.*

- The Vs of big data (volume, velocity, variety; veracity as the added fourth), and which one a described failure exhibits <!-- obj 1 · Understand · Moodle MC -->
- Order and roles of map / shuffle / reduce (which phase moves data?) <!-- obj 2 · Analyze · Moodle MC -->
- What lazy evaluation defers, and which calls trigger execution <!-- obj 3 · Apply · Moodle MC -->

## FAQ / Industry Reality

**"Is Hadoop dead, then?"** — The *programming model* (hand-written MapReduce jobs) is
legacy; the *ideas* won completely. HDFS-style distributed storage underlies modern data
lakes, and Spark, which replaced it at the compute layer, is itself a MapReduce
descendant — today usually rented as a managed service (Databricks, AWS EMR, Google
Dataproc) rather than self-hosted. Learn the paradigm, not the brand.

**"My 50 GB CSV — is that big data?"** — Almost certainly not. Convert it to Parquet
(L03), point DuckDB at it (L02), and it's an interactive dataset on one machine. "Doesn't
fit in Excel" is not the bar; "doesn't fit on a machine" is.

## Deep Dive: RDDs vs DataFrames (Optional)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Click to expand: RDDs vs DataFrames"}
Spark's original API, the **RDD** (Resilient Distributed Dataset), exposes map and
reduce directly over arbitrary Python objects — maximum control, no optimizer help. The
**DataFrame** API you use in the lab describes *what* you want in named-column
operations, letting Spark's Catalyst optimizer reorder, prune, and fuse the work (the
`.explain()` plan is its output). The relationship mirrors L02: RDDs are the imperative
loop, DataFrames the declarative query. Modern Spark code is DataFrames almost
exclusively; RDDs surface only when structure truly disappears.
:::

## Where to Go Deeper

- **Official docs:** [Spark — Quick Start](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/quick-start.html) — the lab's API surface, one page.
- **Official docs:** [Spark — Cluster Mode Overview](https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/cluster-overview.html) — driver and executors, with the diagram.
- **The classic paper:** [Dean & Ghemawat (2004), *MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters*](https://research.google/pubs/mapreduce-simplified-data-processing-on-large-clusters/) — readable, and the census analogy is basically its Figure 1.
